retrieved_docs.title: Prosopagnosia -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Prosopagnosia is defined as the inability to recognize known and new faces. It is also known as facial/visual agnosia. [1] Bodamer first used the word prosopagnosia in 1947 in a landmark paper that described the cases of two patients with face recognition deficits. [2] The word comes from Greek prosopon, meaning face and agnosia, meaning lack of knowledge. Normally, an individual can recognize and remember 5000+ faces throughout their lifetime. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Prosopagnosia -- Clinical Significance -- Neuropsychological Diagnostic Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic tests can divide into three main types. First, perception tests that can accurately evaluate the patient's ability to discriminate between different visual facial stimuli. Second, recognition tests that can specifically assess short and long term recognition patterns of faces. Third, facial identification tests that involve other auditory or tactile cues (such as naming) to help identify facial stimuli. Examples for face perception tests are the Cambridge Face Perception Test, Glasgow Face Matching Test, Benton Facial Recognition, and the Caledonian Face Test. [8] [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Stereognosis -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: To properly understand the clinical relevance of stereognosis, one must first appreciate the primary pathway that allows perception of the size and shape of the object, the DCMLT, and how this information is processed in the parietal cortex.

retrieved_docs.title: Prosopagnosia -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: There are varying degrees of impairment in prosopagnosia, including: The inability to recognize Discriminate Identify different or own faces Discern differences between faces and surrounding objects.

retrieved_docs.title: Agnosia -- Introduction -- Types of Agnosia
retrieved_docs.content: Prosopagnosia is the inability to recognize familiar faces. Patients can often identify other aspects like gender, hair, and emotions. Prosopagnosia results from damage to the fusiform face area (located in the inferior temporal cortex in the fusiform gyrus). People with apperceptive prosopagnosia cannot perceive facial expressions and cues but recognize non-facial clues like hair and clothing. Associative prosopagnosia patients can derive some facial information like gender and age. Mr. P in Oliver Sack’s book The Man Who Mistook His Wife for a Hat presents a classic case of prosopagnosia. Simultanagnosia is the inability to recognize and sort out objects when they appear together, but they can recognize them when they appear alone. Patients cannot perceive the overall meaning of a picture or multiple things together, although they can describe isolated elements. Two forms of simultagnosia have been described.

retrieved_docs.title: Prosopagnosia -- Clinical Significance -- Localization/Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The pathophysiology is still not completely understood and is a prominent area of research. Damage or developmental anomaly in the right or bilateral fusiform-lingual gyrus, with neuron pathways that control facial perception and memory, are thought to be involved. Imaging research shows that deficits in the temporal cortex and amygdala can also be involved. Individuals with fusiform lesions are more likely to have an apperceptive variant, while those with anterior temporal lobe lesions have the amnesia variant. Development prosopagnosia appears to be a result of either a disconnection between the anterior and posterior face networks or reduced functional activation of regions responsible for facial recognition and identification. Advanced imaging studies support these concepts. The hypothesis is that dysfunctional neural migration during development may be the mechanism behind juvenile prosopagnosia. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Prosopagnosia -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: To compensate for their deficit, patients use their other senses and cues such as voice, shapes, and anomalous contours of the face. These compensatory mechanisms, at times, are not enough to recognize familiar faces. This impairment causes a psychological and social impact leading to functional impairment, unemployment, social isolation, depression, anxiety, and other mood disorders. [4] Peculiarly, patients complain of having trouble following television shows and movies because they cannot visually keep track of characters.

retrieved_docs.title: Prosopagnosia -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Variants of prosopagnosia include an apperceptive variant (deficits in facial structure perception), and amnestic or associative variant (unable to remember faces even though they can perceive them; the perceptual information can not access facial memories because of a disconnection or loss of them). [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Prosopagnosia -- Clinical Significance -- Neuropsychological Diagnostic Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Facial memories are testable through imagery. [11] Other tools include self-report questionnaires, including the Cambridge Face Memory Questionnaire, Kennerknecht 15-item questionnaire, and the 20-item Prosopagnosia Index. These instruments of research tend to have low sensitivity and specificity for diagnosing prosopagnosia; therefore, objective measures should be pursued.

retrieved_docs.title: Cognitive Assessment -- Definition/Introduction -- Visuospatial skills
retrieved_docs.content: This concept is a person’s ability to conceptualize and manipulate 2- and 3-dimensional objects. Testing is often done by copying figures, block designs, or clock drawings. [17] This skill set may be difficult to assess while taking a history. Still, it could present as a patient suddenly having difficulty with parallel parking their car or getting into small accidents. [9] In neuropsychology, an example of a test used for these skills is the Rey-Osterrieth Complex Figure Copy Test. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Stereognosis -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Manual stereognosis requires intact peripheral sensory pathways, namely the dorsal column-medial lemniscus tract (DCMLT), to receive discriminative touch and proprioceptive information. Receival of this information is necessary but not sufficient for stereognosis because it also requires functioning processing centers in the cortex of the parietal lobe. See Image. The Sensory Tract.

retrieved_docs.title: Cortical Blindness -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Differential diagnoses of cortical blindness are: Hemineglect Prosopagnosia Simultagnosia Malingering

retrieved_docs.title: Stereognosis -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Stereognosis is the ability to identify the shape and form of a three-dimensional object and, therefore, its identity with tactile manipulation of that object in the absence of visual and auditory stimuli. The etymology of the word stereognosis is from the Greek for "stereo," meaning solid, and "gnosis," meaning knowledge. A distinction is necessary between manual and oral stereognosis, which occurs in the hands and mouth. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Agnosia -- Introduction -- Types of Agnosia
retrieved_docs.content: Akinetopsia refers to the inability to perceive motion.

retrieved_docs.title: Agnosia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: For example, to assess for visual agnosia, show the patient several familiar items like keys, a pen, and a banana and ask to name them. Differentiating visual agnosia and aphasia is important. For prosopagnosia, show the patient pictures of famous people, their family members, and themselves. For color agnosia, show solid color cards and ask them to name the colors. For simultagnosia, present pictures with several objects or people and ask them to identify what they see. For tactile agnosia, make sure the patient cannot see, then put common things in each hand 1 by 1 and ask the patient to identify them by touch alone. The inability to correctly identify at least 50% of stimuli tested suggests the diagnosis of agnosia. [1] [2] [3] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Agnosia -- Introduction -- Types of Agnosia
retrieved_docs.content: Ventral simultagnosia: These individuals cannot identify more than one object or complex object at a time, although they can see more than one object at a time. They cannot perceive the whole picture as 1 and derive meaning from it. For example, in a night sky picture with stars and a full moon, they may identify the moon as a ball, unable to derive the meaning of the whole picture. Ventral simultagnosia is associated with lesions in the left inferior occipital area.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2913
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier denotes that specific clinical manifestations are recognized as relevant descriptors in the context of mozkomíšní mok. In particular, blindness and vision defects are identified as permissible qualifiers associated with mozkomíšní mok, indicating that visual impairment may be a notable feature of this condition. Likewise, impulse control disorders are also listed as allowed qualifiers for mozkomíšní mok, reflecting that disturbances in behavioral regulation can be linked to this entity. By acknowledging both blindness and vision defects and impulse control disorders as allowed qualifiers, clinicians are guided to consider a broader spectrum of neurological and psychiatric presentations when evaluating patients with abnormalities of mozkomíšní mok.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 104
retrieved_docs.content: and organic mental disorders function as an overarching category encompassing mood, anxiety, miscellaneous, and schizophrenia/psychotic disorders, and include specific Arabic diagnoses such as حالة ارتباكية دون الحادة (sub‑acute confusional state), خرف وعائي مصحوب حالة مزاجية اكتئابية (vascular dementia with depressive mood), ذُهان شيخوخي (senile psychosis), ضمور المخ المحدد (focal brain atrophy), and متلازمة الهلوسة العضوية (organic hallucination syndrome). Clinical signs such as حركات متكررة نمطية and تبلد المشاعر are identified as manifestations of ضمور المخ المحدد. Within this framework, the transient organic psychotic condition, depressive type, overlaps with mood disorders. Personal attributes related to sexuality are captured by sexuální orientace (sexual orientation), interpreted through residual and unclassified codes, linked inversely to the broader Personal Attribute concept, and associated with تحول جنسي (gender transition). Genderová identita (gender identity) maintains a

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: الحالة المزاجية العضوي while also relating to اضطراب نفسي عضوي عابر وغير محدد (unspecified transient organic psychotic disorder), illustrating fluid boundaries among organic mood, psychotic, and miscellaneous categories. Neurodegeneration, exemplified by ضمور المخ المحدد, is linked to حركات متكررة نمطية (stereotypic repetitive movements) and تبلد المشاعر (emotional blunting). These manifestations are associated with Genderová identita (gender identity), suggesting that specific brain atrophy can influence both motor stereotypies and affective flattening, thereby affecting gender‑identity expression. Overall, the taxonomy interconnects drug‑induced and physiologic anxiety and mood disorders, organic psychotic states, sexual‑orientation–related behaviors, post‑concussion and senescence syndromes, and neurodegenerative manifestations, supporting consistent diagnostic reasoning and coordinated patient care across psychiatric and neurological conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2916
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of an “Allowed qualifier” is linked to both “blindness and vision defects” and to “mozkomíšní mok” (cerebrospinal fluid), indicating that this qualifier can be applied when describing visual impairments that may be associated with abnormalities of the cerebrospinal fluid. In turn, “mozkomíšní mok” is connected to “إجْهاضٌ مَصْحُوبٌ بعَدْوَى” (abortion accompanied by infection), suggesting that disturbances in cerebrospinal fluid can also be relevant in the context of infectious complications of pregnancy loss. Together, these relationships imply that the allowed qualifier serves as a bridge for describing clinical scenarios where visual deficits, cerebrospinal fluid pathology, and infection‑related abortion intersect, highlighting the importance of recognizing how neurological and reproductive systems may be interrelated in complex medical cases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1603
retrieved_docs.content: The term خلل الوظيفة الجنسية (“sexual dysfunction”) is classified as a type of خَطَل جنسي (“sexual disorder”). Within the broader category of خلل الوظيفة الجنسية are several specific conditions: اضطراب الهوية الجنسية (“gender identity disorder”), الاسْتِعْراء (“sexual exploitation”), and الميل الجنسي إلى الأطفال (“pedophilic sexual interest”). The concept قسّاح is also identified as a child entity of خلل الوظيفة الجنسية, indicating it is a more specific manifestation within the sexual dysfunction spectrum. Additionally, the Czech phrase sexuální dysfunkce fyziologická (“physiological sexual dysfunction”) is mapped from خلل الوظيفة الجنسية, showing that the two expressions refer to the same clinical phenomenon in different languages.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 182
retrieved_docs.content: this anticholinergic syndrome, indicating shared anticholinergic mechanisms. Miscellaneous mental health disorders are primarily derived from اضطراب الحالة المزاجية العضوي (organic mood disorder) and maintain a broader relationship with اضطراب نفسي عضوي عابر وغير محدد (transient and unspecified organic psychotic disorder), encompassing a range of organic psychiatric presentations. Genderová identita (gender identity) is reported to be associated with حركات متكررة نمطية (stereotypic repetitive movements), and these movements are manifestations of ضمور المخ المحدد (specific brain atrophy). Likewise, تبلد المشاعر (emotional blunting) is also a manifestation of ضمور المخ المحدد. Together, these connections demonstrate how anticholinergic effects, structural brain atrophy, and various organic mental syndromes interrelate, informing clinical assessment and management of patients presenting with cognitive, behavioral, and emotional disturbances.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 0 -- Community 20
retrieved_docs.content: The community’s knowledge organizes behavioral and psychiatric phenomena under the Arabic‑language residual‑code “الهَذَيان” (psychosis) and the sexual‑behavior residual‑code “فرط الجنس” (hypersexuality), both children of the broader “SEXUAL STATES AND ORIENTATIONS” group. “الهَذَيان” contains the child concept “حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة” (transient organic psychotic states), a subset of “ذُهان عضوي” (organic psychosis) that includes non‑infectious encephalitis (“التهاب الدماغ غير المعدي (SMQ)”), psychosis and psychotic disorders (“الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ)”), and non‑infectious delirium/brain disorder (“الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي (SMQ)”). Associated mental symptoms comprise personality problems (“probleem; persoonlijkheid”), anhedonia (“فقد المتعة”), and self‑destructive behavior (“السلوك التدميري الذاتي”). Psychiatric and behavioral disorders related to sexual development are classified in Dutch as “Psychische stoornissen en gedragsstoornissen samenhangend met

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2574
retrieved_docs.content: Emotional blunting (تبلد المشاعر) is identified as a clinical manifestation of specific brain atrophy (ضمور المخ المحدد). This relationship indicates that patients who exhibit localized cerebral atrophy may present with reduced emotional responsiveness, and recognizing emotional blunting as a sign of such atrophy can aid clinicians in diagnosing underlying neurological conditions where affective changes are a prominent feature.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1886
retrieved_docs.content: Behavioral/psychiatric manifestations are classified as a subcategory of the broader entity الهَذَيان, indicating that these mental and behavioral signs are considered part of the larger domain represented by الهَذَيان. This hierarchical relationship helps organize clinical features, allowing clinicians to recognize that behavioral and psychiatric presentations fall within the overarching concept of الهَذَيان and to consider them in the context of that broader classification when assessing and managing patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1826
retrieved_docs.content: The category “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” is presented as a parent concept that includes the specific child entity “غشي.” In this framework, the general term “Symptoms” is described as closely related to, and potentially synonymous with, the broader phrase “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” indicating that the two expressions refer to overlapping clinical manifestations. Consequently, the specific manifestation “غشي” is understood to fall under the umbrella of nonspecific or ill‑defined symptomatology, and clinicians should recognize its inclusion within this broader symptom category when evaluating and documenting patient presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 142
retrieved_docs.content: SEXUAL STATES AND ORIENTATIONS is classified under residual codes; unclassified and includes the child concept فرط الجنس (hypersexuality). The broader category Psychische stoornissen en gedragsstoornissen samenhangend met seksuele ontwikkeling en oriëntatie is also placed under residual codes; unclassified, indicating that both sexual state categories and related psychiatric and behavioral disorders are considered residual or unclassified conditions. Within this framework, the predicate is_interpreted_by links residual codes; unclassified to sexuální orientace, suggesting that sexual orientation provides an interpretive context for these residual codes. Personal Attribute is a generic attribute class that encompasses both porodní hmotnost (birth weight) and sexuální orientace, indicating that sexual orientation is regarded as a type of personal attribute alongside physiological measures such as birth weight. Genderová identita (gender identity) is directly related to sexuální orientace, reflecting the clinical observation that gender identity and sexual orientation are interrelated aspects of a person’s identity. Genderová identita also shows a relationship with حركات متكررة نمطية (stereotyped repetitive movements), which are identified as a manifestation_of ضمور المخ المحدد (specific brain atrophy). Similarly, تبلد المشاعر (emotional blunting) is another manifestation_of ضمور المخ المحدد, highlighting that specific brain atrophy can present with both motor stereotypies and affective flattening. Finally, sexuální orientace is noted to use تحول جنسي (gender transition), indicating that concepts of sexual orientation are relevant in the context of gender transition procedures or discussions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 246
retrieved_docs.content: (حركات متكررة نمطية). These movements, together with emotional blunting (تبلد المشاعر), are described as manifestations of a specific form of brain atrophy (ضمور المخ المحدد). Additionally, organic psychosis (ذُهان عضوي) is related to schizophrenic‑like manic symptoms (هوس شَبَقِيّ), indicating a connection between organic psychotic processes and manic presentations of schizophrenia. Overall, the knowledge structure connects sexual orientation and related psychosexual disorders with broader residual and unspecified mental‑health categories, integrates personal attributes and gender identity, and links neuro‑biological findings such as specific brain atrophy to behavioral and emotional manifestations, emphasizing the importance of integrated assessment and classification in clinical practice.

Personal Attribute is defined as porodní hmotnost (birth weight) and, through an inverse_isa relationship, is recognized as a broader class that includes sexuální orientace (sexual orientation). Genderová identita (gender identity) has a relationship with sexuální orientace, emphasizing the clinical relevance of distinguishing gender identity from sexual orientation. Sexuální orientace is noted to use تحول جنسي (gender transition) and is interpreted by residual codes; unclassified, suggesting that residual coding schemes are applied to interpret variations in sexual orientation. Oppositional Defiant Disorder, expressed in Dutch as “solitair; agressief type gedragsstoornis,” corresponds to the Arabic term اضطراب المعارض المتحدي. Genderová identita also has a relationship with حركات متكررة نمطية (repetitive stereotyped movements), which are manifestations of ضمور المخ المحدد (focal brain atrophy). The same focal brain atrophy underlies تبلد المشاعر (emotional

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 93
retrieved_docs.content: hallucinations within the same pharmacologically related syndrome. Together, these relationships illustrate how specific organic psychiatric conditions and syndromes are organized under broader diagnostic categories and pharmacological classifications, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, treatment planning, and understanding of underlying pathophysiology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 1030
retrieved_docs.content: Hemiplegia/hemiparesis is classified as a subtype of paralysis, indicating that loss of movement on one side of the body falls under the broader category of paralytic disorders. The condition “MIGRAINE, FAMILIAL HEMIPLEGIC, 2” can present with the manifestation حُبسة, a symptom that is grouped within the set of الحالات ذات الصلة بالنَزْف في الجهاز العصبي المركزي والأمراض الوعائية الدماغية (SMQ), which comprises disorders related to central nervous system hemorrhage and cerebrovascular disease. This same SMQ group also includes paralysis as one of its members, linking hemiplegic manifestations to vascular and hemorrhagic brain pathologies. In clinical terminology, paralysis is interpreted through the concept of pohyb (movement), and the movement framework (pohyb) is also used to interpret باركنسونية ثانوية (secondary parkinsonism), highlighting a shared interpretive model for motor dysfunction across different neurological conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2594
retrieved_docs.content: The CELLOSAURUS Disease Terminology includes the concept شقيقة, and the Neurological Disorders (PsycINFO Subcluster Term) lists شقيقة as one of its members, indicating that شقيقة is recognized as a neurological disorder within both the CELLOSAURUS classification system and the PsycINFO subcluster of neurological conditions.
